10.1016/j.jmp.2016.02.002
The results described here and the title of our paper were inspired by some of Duncan Luce’s work, and specifically, by the invariance principle that he called a ‘Principle of Theory Construction’ in his well known paper “ On the possible psychophysical laws ” ( Luce, 1959 ). His idea was to link the ‘admissible transformations’ of the variables in a scientific equation to the possible form of the scientific law formalized by that equation, with ‘admissible transformations’ meaning ‘changes of units’. This paper was widely cited, in part because it was controversial, but also because it was highly suggestive of a new approach to the formal analysis of scientific laws from a foundational standpoint.

In any event, Luce’s paper was harshly criticized by  Rozeboom (1962a)  (see also   Luce, 1962; Rozeboom, 1962b , for the ensuing exchange between them). The difficulty is that Luce’s statement of his principle was ambiguous and susceptible to several interpretations, some of them disputable.

In a subsequent paper,  Luce (1964)  attempted to give a mathematically clearer interpretation of the principle, and one that included multiple input variables. His approach, which was extended by  Aczél, Roberts, and Rosenbaum (1986) ,  Kim (1990) , and  Osborne (1970) , goes as follows.

Note in passing that, as a consequence of his exchange with Rozeboom (cf.   Luce, 1962; Rozeboom, 1962b ), Duncan Luce no longer referred to the ‘Principle of Theory Construction’ as a “principle”. Rather, he thought such a “principle” should be “ a result derived from deeper principles of theory construction ” (cf. personal communication from Luce to Aczél, and see   Aczél et al., 1986 , page 390).

In their papers,  Aczél et al. (1986) ,  Kim (1990) ,  Luce (1964)  and  Osborne (1970)  analyze the functional equation  (3)  under various hypotheses concerning the function  
                         u 
                       and the scale types–ratio, interval, ordinal, or log-interval–of the independent and dependent variables.

We have two concerns about this approach. One has to do with the suitability of Eq.  (1)  as an interpretation of Luce’s Principle. In the usual way science is conducted, the units of the measurement scales are not part of the formal notation and marked in the equations. They may be indicated in a parenthetical comment, or they may not. The Pythagorean Theorem or Newton’s Law of Universal Gravitation is always stated without any mention of the units. 
                         1 
                      
                      
                         1 
                         In the Law of Gravity, the constant  
                               G 
                             in  
                               F 
                               = 
                               G 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           1 
                                        
                                     
                                     
                                        
                                           m 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     
                                        
                                           r 
                                        
                                        
                                           2 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                             stands for units, but the units themselves are rarely mentioned. 
                       This is sensible because the units of the scales have no representation in nature. 
                         2 
                      
                      
                         2 
                         The only exception is the counting measure, in the Avogadro number. 
                       So, any scientific equation whose form would fundamentally depend upon the units of the variables would be a poor representation of a phenomenon. But this leads us to a different interpretation of Luce’s Principle:  
                         
                            The mathematical form of an equation formalizing a scientific law should be invariant under change of the units of its variables ; 
                       or in the jargon of this paper:  
                         
                            The equation should be meaningful . 
                       Eqs.  (1) and (3) , while not obviously inconsistent with this ‘meaningfulness’ principle, are not faithful representations of it: nothing indicates that  
                         
                            
                               T 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            
                               
                                  x 
                               
                               
                                  i 
                               
                            
                            ) 
                         
                       represents a change of unit compared to  
                         
                            
                               x 
                            
                            
                               i 
                            
                         
                      . This will be made clear by a comparison with a similar ‘meaningfulness’ equation at the core of our paper. 
                         3 
                      
                      
                         3 
                         Compare Eq.  (3)  with Eq.  (29) ; see Section   7 . 
                       We continue this discussion in our last section.

Our second concern is the focus on equations involving interval or log-interval scales. Equations with such scale types were studied in the past with an eye toward application in the social sciences, and perhaps for mathematical generality, but looking with a more modern lens after having seen the progress, we maintain that the focus on equations involving these scale types seems overblown. Indeed, most of the laws of physics or geometry are expressed in terms of ratio scales. So, our understanding of the principles of the construction of laws relating ratio scale variables should perhaps have priority.

In this paper, following  Doble (2002) ,  Falmagne (2004, 2015) ,  Falmagne and Doble (2015) ,  Falmagne and Narens (1983) , and  Narens (2002, 2007) , we formalize a concept of meaningfulness in which the units of the variables are explicitly represented in the notation. 
                         4 
                      
                      
                         4 
                         The resulting notation is rather heavy. But this is temporary. We can return to the standard notation once the results of the meaningfulness assumption have been obtained. 
                       Moreover, we restrict consideration to functions of ratio scale variables producing ratio scale values. We call such a function a ‘code.’ As these basic hypotheses, once properly formalized, are quite strong, relatively weak, intuitive, possibly obvious, additional assumptions may be needed to obtain the exact mathematical form of a scientific or geometric law. This paper contains three examples of such derivations.

The next section is devoted to some preparatory concepts and results. Section   3  contains a definition of the meaningfulness condition, including some examples. In Section   4 , we show that a new derivation of the Pythagorean Theorem can be obtained, assuming that the meaningfulness condition is satisfied and that the code representing the hypothenuse of a right triangle is an associative, homogeneous function of the length of the two sides. Our second example, in Section   5 , is in the same vein, and derives Beer’s Law from meaningfulness and the hypothesis that the code satisfies a functional equation condition called ‘translatability’. (We only sketch the proofs of the results in Sections   4 and 5 , which were obtained by  Falmagne, 2015 .) In Section   6 , we suppose that the codes are monomials, having each of their variables representable as a monomial whose variables belong to a basis. 
                         5 
                      
                      
                         5 
                         This is familiar terrain. In many physics contexts, for example, we can take as a basis measurement ‘dimensions’ such as mass, length, and time. Denoting these by

                                  M

                             for energy. 
                       We show how we could derive the exact form of such monomials, including the exponents. Such a result is quite similar to some dimensional analysis derivations. In our case, however, we shall see that it results from an application of the meaningfulness condition. In our final Section   7 , we go back to our discussion of Luce’s ‘Principle of Theory Construction’, with a comparison between the  Luce (1964)  interpretation as Eq.  (1)  and the corresponding meaningfulness equation.

This fundamental property of algebra usually applies to a binary operation. We shall see in  Theorem 9  that for meaningful collections of ‘self-transforming’ codes,

We motivate the definition of meaningfulness by the example of Beer’s Law, already mentioned in this paper. This law, which is also called the Beer–Lambert law or the Bouguer–Beer law, applies in a class of empirical situations where an incident radiation traverses some medium, such as an absorbing liquid, so that only a fraction of the radiation goes through, the relation between the incident radiation and the output being exponential. The mathematical expression of Beer’s Law is  
                         
                            (9) 
                            
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               x 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     e 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           c 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        + 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  c 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        + 
                                        + 
                                     
                                  
                                  constant 
                                  ) 
                               
                               , 
                            
                         
                       in which  
                         x 
                       denotes the intensity of the incident radiation,  
                         y 
                       is the concentration of the absorbing medium, which might be measured by the length of a tube containing the absorbing liquid,  
                         c 
                       is a reference level (such as the upper bound of the values of  
                         y 
                      ), and  
                         I 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                       is the intensity of the transmitted radiation. 
                         7 
                      
                      
                         7 
                         The reader will also recognize Eq.  (9)  as giving the law of exponential decay, with  
                               x 
                             being an initial amount,  
                               y 
                             an elapsed time, and  
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ) 
                               
                             a final amount.

The trouble with the notation of Eq.  (9)  is its ambiguity: the units of  
                         x 
                       and  
                         y 
                       are not specified. Writing  
                         I 
                         
                            ( 
                            70 
                            , 
                            3 
                            ) 
                         
                       has no empirical meaning if one does not specify, for example, that 70 and 3 refer to 70 candelas and 3 m, respectively. Such a parenthetical reference is standard in a scientific context, but is not instrumental for our purpose, which is to express, formally, an invariance with respect to any change in the units. 
                         8 
                      
                      
                         8 
                         A relevant point is made by Suppes; see “Why the Fundamental Equations of Physical Theories are not Invariant”, page 120 of  Suppes (2002) .

To rectify the ambiguity, we propose to interpret  
                         I 
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                       as a shorthand notation for  
                         
                            
                               I 
                            
                            
                               1 
                               , 
                               1 
                            
                         
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                      , in which  
                         x 
                       and  
                         I 
                       on the one hand, and  
                         y 
                       on the other hand, are measured in terms of two conventional  initial  or  anchor  units fixed arbitrarily. Such units could be the  candela  and  meter , if one wishes. The  
                         
                            
                            
                               1 
                               , 
                               1 
                            
                         
                       indices of  
                         
                            
                               I 
                            
                            
                               1 
                               , 
                               1 
                            
                         
                       signify these initial units. Describing the phenomenon in terms of other units amounts to multiplying  
                         x 
                       and  
                         y 
                       in any pair  
                         
                            ( 
                            x 
                            , 
                            y 
                            ) 
                         
                       by some positive constants  
                         α 
                       and   
                         β 
                      , respectively. At the same time,  
                         I 
                       also gets to be multiplied by  
                         α 
                      , and the reference level  
                         c 
                       by   
                         β 
                      . Doing so defines a new function  
                         
                            
                               I 
                            
                            
                               α 
                               , 
                               β 
                            
                         
                      , which is different from  
                         I 
                         = 
                         
                            
                               I 
                            
                            
                               1 
                               , 
                               1 
                            
                         
                       if either  
                         α 
                         ≠ 
                         1 
                       or  
                         β 
                         ≠ 
                         1 
                      . For example, if our new units are  1000 candelas  and  millimeter , then the two expressions  
                         
                            
                               
                                  
                                     I 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                     
                                        
                                           0 
                                        
                                        
                                           − 
                                           3 
                                        
                                     
                                     , 
                                     
                                        
                                           10 
                                        
                                        
                                           3 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  0.07 
                                  , 
                                  3000 
                                  ) 
                               
                               
                               and 
                               
                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  70 
                                  , 
                                  3 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               
                                  
                                     I 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                     , 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  70 
                                  , 
                                  3 
                                  ) 
                               
                               , 
                            
                         
                       while numerically not equal, describe the same empirical situation.

This points to the appropriate definition of  
                         
                            
                               I 
                            
                            
                               α 
                               , 
                               β 
                            
                         
                      . We should write:  
                         
                            (10) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     I 
                                  
                                  
                                     α 
                                     , 
                                     β 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ) 
                               
                               = 
                               x 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     e 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     
                                        
                                           y 
                                        
                                        
                                           β 
                                           c 
                                        
                                     
                                  
                               
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x 
                                  , 
                                  y 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        + 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                  c 
                                  ∈ 
                                  
                                     
                                        R 
                                     
                                     
                                        + 
                                        + 
                                     
                                  
                                  constant 
                                  ) 
                               
                               . 
                            
                         
                       The connection between  
                         I 
                       and  
                         
                            
                               I 
                            
                            
                               α 
                               , 
                               β 
                            
                         
                       is thus  
                         
                            (11) 
                            
                               
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                                  
                                     α 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  
                                     I 
                                  
                                  
                                     α

                                     β 
                                  
                               
                               
                                  ( 
                                  α

                                  ( 
                                  
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                     
                                        α 
                                     
                                  
                                  ) 
                               
                               α 
                               x 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     e

                                           β

                                           β

                               = 
                               x 
                               
                               
                                  
                                     e

                               I 
                               
                                  ( 
                                  x

                               .

                         α 
                         ,

                         ,

                               F

In the next two sections, we derive some exemplary results combining the meaningfulness condition with some abstract axioms, in particular, associativity and translatability. These results are due to  Falmagne (2015) . We only give a sketch of the proofs.

Here, we have only one measurement scale, which is the same for the two input variables, the lengths of the two sides of the right triangle, and for the output variable, the length of the hypothenuse. We require the collection of codes

This theorem is essentially the Pythagorean theorem. The associativity of the hypothenuse of a right triangle, as a function of the length of the two sides, can be proved by a simple geometric argument (cf. Figure 1 in   Falmagne, 2015 ). The fact that we must have  
                         θ 
                         = 
                         2 
                       can be derived from the Area of the Square Postulate. 
                         9 
                      
                      
                         9 
                         The area of a square with side length  
                               s 
                             is equal to  
                               
                                  
                                     s 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                            . 
                      
                      
                         Sketch of proof 
                         Without loss of generality, we suppose that the initial code  
                               F 
                             is homogeneous and associative. Using meaningfulness and homogeneity, this implies that any code  
                               
                                  
                                     F 
                                  
                                  
                                     α 
                                  
                               
                             in  
                               F 
                             is identical to  
                               F 
                            . From  Lemma 4  applied to the initial code   
                               F 
                            , there exists a continuous, strictly monotonic function  
                               f 
                             such that, by homogeneity again,  
                               
                                  (20) 
                                  
                                     F 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        α 
                                        x 
                                        , 
                                        α 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     f

                                              f 
                                           
                                           
                                              − 
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           α 
                                           x 
                                           )

                                              f 
                                           
                                           
                                              − 
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           α 
                                           y 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     α 
                                     f 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        
                                           
                                              f 
                                           
                                           
                                              − 
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           x 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        + 
                                        
                                           
                                              f 
                                           
                                           
                                              − 
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           ( 
                                           y 
                                           ) 
                                        
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     = 
                                     α 
                                     F 
                                     
                                        ( 
                                        x 
                                        , 
                                        y 
                                        ) 
                                     
                                     . 
                                  
                               
                             Applying  
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             on both sides of the second equation, we can transform this equation first into a Cauchy equation, and next into a Pexider equation  
                               
                                  
                                     f 
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     1

Requiring that a collection of codes is meaningful has the important consequence that many conditions imposed on the initial code automatically propagate to all the other codes of the collection. This can be shown for the associativity and translatability conditions used in this paper. The  Falmagne (2015)  paper contains many other examples of such a propagation, such as permutability, quasi-permutability, and differentiability.

This suggests an application to dimensional analysis. For instance, let the initial code of a meaningful collection

                         F 
                      . We devote this section to the relevant definitions and results in the context of meaningfulness.

The terse monomials can be taken as formalizations of a possible type of scientific law. They have been axiomatized in the style of our  Theorems 9 and 10  (see   Falmagne, 2004, 2015 ). Here, however, we assume that we are given a meaningful collection of monomial codes, and our task is to compute the exponents

                         ≤ 
                         n

                         n 
                       variables.

Theorem 15 
                         
                            Any finite set of terse monomials has a basis, which may not be unique.

Any finite set of terse monomials has a basis, which may not be unique.

We now give a simple example of a monomial law with two input variables that demonstrates  Example 16  and  Theorem 17 .

Example 18 
                         Consider the relationship among electric power, voltage (electric potential), and electrical resistance. We can take the basis to be  
                               
                                  { 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        1 
                                     
                                  
                                  , 
                                  
                                     
                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  } 
                               
                            , where  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             is electric power (whose SI unit is the watt,  
                               
                                  kg 
                               
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        m 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                               ⋅ 
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        s 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     3 
                                  
                               
                            ) and  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                             is current (whose SI unit is the ampere,  
                               A 
                            ). In the notation of  Example 16  and  Theorem 17 , we have the following:  
                               
                                  
                                     (68) 
                                     
                                        
                                           power 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        y 
                                        = 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                        = 
                                        κ 
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    ρ 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    1 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    ρ 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    2 
                                                 
                                              
                                           
                                        
                                        , 
                                        with   
                                        κ 
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              ρ 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        = 
                                        1 
                                        , 
                                        
                                           
                                              ρ 
                                           
                                           
                                              2 
                                           
                                        
                                        = 
                                        0 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     (69) 
                                     
                                        
                                           voltage 
                                        
                                        = 
                                        
                                           
                                              z 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        = 
                                        
                                           
                                              κ 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                        
                                        
                                           
                                              x 
                                           
                                           
                                              1 
                                           
                                           
                                              
                                                 
                                                    γ 
                                                 
                                                 
                                                    1 
                                                    , 
                                                    1

Remark 19 
                         
                            Theorem 17  can be used to uncover the values of the parameters  
                               ϕ 
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     ψ 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                             of a monomial law represented by Eq.  (55)  when we know the numerical values of the parameters of the basic representations in Eqs.  (56) and (57) . Sometimes, only some of these values may be accessible, such as the exponents, as in the following example.

Theorem 17  can be used to uncover the values of the parameters  
                               ϕ 
                             and  
                               
                                  
                                     ψ 
                                  
                                  
                                     j 
                                  
                               
                             of a monomial law represented by Eq.  (55)  when we know the numerical values of the parameters of the basic representations in Eqs.  (56) and (57) . Sometimes, only some of these values may be accessible, such as the exponents, as in the following example.

Example 20 
                         Kepler’s Third Law 
                         Suppose we are interested in relating the period  
                               T 
                             and the length  
                               a 
                             of the semi-major axis of an elliptical orbit (in which a celestial body is orbiting a much larger one). A relevant variable is the standard gravitational parameter  
                               μ 
                            , which varies from body to body and has SI units of  
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        m 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     3 
                                  
                               
                               
                               
                                  
                                     
                                        s 
                                     
                                  
                                  
                                     − 
                                     2

                                        x

                                        x 
                                     
                                     
                                        2 
                                     
                                  
                                  }

                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     1 
                                  
                               
                             being length and  
                               
                                  
                                     x 
                                  
                                  
                                     2 
                                  
                               
                             being time. Again using the notation of  Example 16  and  Theorem 17 , we have:

Originally, Duncan Luce interpreted his concept of linking the admissible transformations of the variables (which arises from their scale types) to the possible forms of a scientific law as a ‘Principle of Theory Construction.’ However, due to Rozeboom’s criticisms ( Rozeboom, 1962a ), 
                         10 
                      
                      
                         10 
                         See also  Luce (1962)  and  Rozeboom (1962b) . 
                       he abandoned this idea and ventured that a ‘Principle of Theory Construction’ should be derived from deeper principles.

As he admits in his response to Rozeboom’s broadside ( Luce, 1962 ), the relevant statements in his 1959 paper ( Luce, 1959 ) were ambiguous. However, they are consistent with the general principle that  
                         
                            [ P ] 
                            
                               the possible forms of a scientific law are constrained by the scale types of the variables, the independent variables and the dependent variable. 
                            
                         
                      
                      Luce (1959) ,  Luce (1964) , and  Rozeboom (1962a)  (see also   Aczél et al., 1986; Kim, 1990; Osborne, 1970 ) gave similar mathematical interpretations to that statement. As indicated in our introductory section, if all the independent variables

                               x 
                            
                            
                               n 
                            
                         
                       are ratio scales and the dependent variable

                               + 
                               1 
                            
                         
                       is also a ratio scale, the  Luce (1964)  interpretation of Principle [P] is in the form of the equation

the possible forms of a scientific law are constrained by the scale types of the variables, the independent variables and the dependent variable.

Our contention is that Eq.  (71)  cannot represent an invariance under change of units, and so, is not a faithful mathematical interpretation of Principle [P], while  (72)  is much closer to what Luce had in mind.

In other words, Luce was wrong to abandon his interpretation of his invariance principle as a ‘Principle of Theory Construction.’ His mathematical interpretation of his principle was mistaken, and so was Rozeboom’s, but not the intuition behind it.
